name: Keshava-Kashmiri Bhatta
dates_notes: Mughal-era; dated by manuscript and reported debates with Caitanya's teacher-line.

lineage_polemical.note: Reported debates with the proto-Gauḍīya line make Keśava-Kāśmīrī a conduit-figure for the Bhedābheda → Acintya-Bhedābheda transition.

core_thesis: Keśava-Kāśmīrī Bhaṭṭa is the late-medieval consolidator of Nimbārka-line *svābhāvika-bhedābheda*, working principally as a sub-commentator and a Bhagavad-Gītā exegete rather than as a doctrinal innovator. He preserves the school's core: *bheda* and *abheda* as intrinsic and equally primordial; the threefold reality of *cit*, *acit*, and *Īśvara* (Kṛṣṇa with Rādhā); the *jīva* as eternal *aṃśa* of Brahman; and *bhakti–prapatti* as the operative path to a liberation that does not erase the *jīva*'s real distinctness. His theological signature is a strongly Kṛṣṇa-centred Vaiṣṇavism with developed Rādhā-doctrine that prefigures motifs the Gauḍīya tradition will later systematise. The reported debate-tradition with Caitanya — preserved in Gauḍīya hagiography — makes him a proximate conduit-figure between the Nimbārka *bhedābheda* line and the emerging *acintya-bhedābheda* of Bengal, whether or not the historical encounter is reconstructible in detail.

engaged_works.title: Krama-Dipika
engaged_works.summary: The *Krama-Dīpikā* is Keśava-Kāśmīrī's manual of Kṛṣṇa-*upāsanā* and *mantra-rahasya*, organised as a graded (*krama*) exposition of the contemplative and ritual practices of the Nimbārka Sanaka-sampradāya. It works through *mantra*, *dhyāna*, *nyāsa*, and the structured *prapatti* sequence by which the practitioner is incorporated into Kṛṣṇa-with-Rādhā worship. Doctrinally, the text presupposes *svābhāvika-bhedābheda*: the *jīva*'s eternal status as *aṃśa* of Brahman is the precondition for *bhakti* being a real, mutual, non-collapsing relation rather than a provisional means whose truth is later cancelled. The work has been used widely outside the Nimbārka line — Gauḍīya practitioners cite it — and is one of the channels by which proto-Caitanya devotional vocabulary develops a continuous receiving lineage. Its Rādhā-tattva, in particular, prefigures motifs that Rūpa and Jīva will later raise to systematic theology.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Prakashika
engaged_works.summary: The *Tattva-Prakāśikā* is Keśava-Kāśmīrī's commentary on the Bhagavad-Gītā from within the Nimbārka *svābhāvika-bhedābheda* tradition. Read across the *Gītā*'s eighteen chapters, it consistently construes Kṛṣṇa as *parabrahman* identical with the Brahman of the Upaniṣads, the *jīva* as eternally distinct-and-non-distinct *aṃśa*, and the *prakṛti–puruṣa* exposition of chapters 7 and 13 as licensing the school's three-real ontology rather than any *māyā*-driven reduction. Verses standardly recruited by Advaita to support *jagat-mithyātva* are re-read so as to preserve world-reality and the genuine ontological standing of the devotee. The polemical horizon includes both Śaṅkara's *bhāṣya* and Bhāskara's *aupādhika* qualification: the former for collapsing difference, the latter for treating it as adventitious. The commentary is the principal Nimbārka-line *Gītā* reading and a key channel for the school's wider influence in early-modern Vaiṣṇavism.

engaged_works.title: Kaustubha-Prabha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-commentary on the Vedānta-Kaustubha.
engaged_works.summary: The *Kaustubha-Prabhā* is Keśava-Kāśmīrī's gloss on Śrīnivāsa's *Vedānta-Kaustubha* — and so a sub-sub-commentary on Nimbārka's *Pārijāta-Saurabha* on the Brahma-Sūtras. It functions as the Nimbārka school's mature scholastic instrument: where Śrīnivāsa supplied the *pūrvapakṣa–uttarapakṣa* architecture absent from Nimbārka's terse bhāṣya, Keśava sharpens the technical defence against the post-Śaṅkara Advaitic mainstream of his time, particularly on the contested *adhikaraṇas* concerning *māyā*, *jīva*-plurality, and *Brahma-pariṇāma*. He preserves the school's signature commitment that *bheda* and *abheda* are equally intrinsic, refusing both Bhāskara's *aupādhika* qualification and any reduction of the *jīva* to mere appearance. The *Kaustubha-Prabhā* is the standard Sanaka-sampradāya reference at the level of fine-grained Vedāntic interpretation and remains the canonical late-medieval statement of the line.
